items.content:
Remember API Monitor? Great tool for tracing Windows app behavior, just left without updates for ages.
Hooking Win32 APIs isn't the hard part. Real nightmare is the endless grunt work. mapping thousands of functions, matching prototypes, and decoding fields. Perfect job to offload to AI.
Getting AI to build it right now to skip the heavy lifting. Release coming soon.

items.author: 0xocdsec

items.content:
For next year’s @reconmtl , I hope no speakers come in and says: “I reversed engineered <insert your platform targets>, spent six month  on it, etc.”

And instead, they say proudly, “I unleashed my AI harness on the binary and it reversed and annotated everything for me, gave me the source code and found <insert quest here>, and wrote a comprehensive report for me. Therefore today I am here just to network and talk about my hobbies , like fishing, bowling, flying FPV drones in acro mode, and occasionally riding my electric unicycle”.  ;)

Truly, RE is so boring as of now. Except for obfuscated targets that need some tooling and more hand holding (for now). But anything else, just “/goal” it and go touch grass and come back. 

I talk from experience. RE for compiler generated code is not fun and is solely a machine job now. If you are still using UIs or manually annotating , renaming or even recovering types, you are doing it wrong. 

That’s why I personally still find pleasure in coding and creating tools, as at least I can be the producer / creative director. Something AI can do, but still you get to pick the creative direction. Like art.

items.content:
RT @OSdev_: Recent UEFI/IOMMU DMA Protection Failures (2025)

Several motherboard vendors were affected by vulnerabilities where firmware claimed DMA protection was enabled but failed to initialize the IOMMU correctly. A malicious PCIe device connected before boot could access memory despite Windows believing protection was active

items.content:
RT @YogSoth0: 🚨 #Langflow Multi-CVE Exploit Kit
💡 **CVE-2026-7524 (Path Traversal) | CVE-2026-7700 (Lambda eval) | CVE-2026-7687 (CodeParser CMD Injection)**  
🧬 **Multi-Vector RCE Exploitation Framework**

😈 **Exploitation Chain:**
1. Create tar.gz with payload file + symlink pointing to target path (e.g., `../../../../tmp/shell.jsp`)
2. Upload to any vulnerable endpoint (`/api/v1/upload/archive`, `/api/v1/docling/`, etc.)
3. Archive extraction follows symlink → writes webshell to web root
4. Access webshell for persistent RCE
#exploit #0days #security #hacking #CVE #CVSS #Langflow #unleaked

items.author: cr3ghost

items.content:
RT @elder_plinius: 🚨 NEW RESEARCH: “Lingua Ex Machina: A Procedural Xenolinguistics Engine Reveals Zero-Shot Language Acquisition, Human-Unreadable Coding Systems, and Exploitable Covert Channels in Frontier AI”

Some of you may remember the name of this lil engine: GLOSSOPETRAE 👅🪨

Well, we've got upgrades 😎

It started as a procedural xenolinguistics engine: one seed in, an entire alien language out. Phonology, morphology, syntax, writing systems, lexicons, grammar docs, all generated from scratch and internally consistent.

Every seed produces a unique language. Every language is deterministic.

Then we used it to ask a weirder question:

Can frontier AI models use languages that never existed before for practical applications?

As it turns out: yes!!

They can read them, write them, translate them, code in them, and even use the weird blind spots between tokenizers as covert channels.

So this paper explores three ideas at once:

▶️ zero-shot language acquisition
▶️ human-unreadable code that models can still execute
▶️ exploitable covert channels in frontier AI systems

GLOSSOPETRAE is no longer just a language generator...
🧵

items.author: alkalinesec

items.content:
C++ has notoriously poor built-in reflection (standard RTTI is slow and limited), almost every major game engine builds its own custom reflection system. This requires generating massive string lookup tables that map string names to object and function pointers.

This is why game engines are so easy to reverse, and if they only use standard RTTI, well that's easy too:

👉 https://t.co/BDTK71qmbN

items.content:
🚨 𝗕𝗲𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝗨𝗽𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗛𝗲𝗹𝗽𝗱𝗲𝘀𝗸 𝗣𝗵𝗶𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗟𝘂𝗿𝗲𝘀 🎣

Got a tip‑off from the 𝗦𝗶𝗹𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗣𝘂𝘀𝗵 𝗦𝗹𝗮𝗰𝗸 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗺𝘂𝗻𝗶𝘁𝘆: a surge of domains like 𝚑𝚎𝚕𝚙𝚍𝚎𝚜𝚔-[𝚌𝚘𝚖𝚙𝚊𝚗𝚢𝚗𝚊𝚖𝚎].𝚡𝚡𝚡 spun up in the past two weeks.

⚠️ Threat actors often let these domains “age” ~30 days before unleashing phishing campaigns.

🛡️ Defenders — add these checks to your hunts for helpdesk‑associated domains:
• 𝗘𝗺𝗮𝗶𝗹𝗘𝘃𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀 → 𝚂𝚎𝚗𝚍𝚎𝚛𝙵𝚛𝚘𝚖𝙰𝚍𝚍𝚛𝚎𝚜𝚜
• 𝗘𝗺𝗮𝗶𝗹𝗨𝗿𝗹𝗜𝗻𝗳𝗼 → 𝚄𝚛𝚕
• 𝗖𝗹𝗼𝘂𝗱𝗔𝗽𝗽𝗘𝘃𝗲𝗻𝘁 → 𝙰𝚌𝚝𝚒𝚘𝚗𝚃𝚢𝚙𝚎=𝚃𝚎𝚊𝚖𝚜𝙸𝚖𝚙𝚎𝚛𝚜𝚘𝚗𝚊𝚝𝚒𝚘𝚗𝙳𝚎𝚝𝚎𝚌𝚝𝚎𝚍 + 𝚁𝚊𝚠𝙴𝚟𝚎𝚗𝚝𝙳𝚊𝚝𝚊.𝚂𝚎𝚗𝚍𝚎𝚛.𝚄𝙿𝙽
• 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗶𝗰𝗲𝗡𝗲𝘁𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸𝗘𝘃𝗲𝗻𝘁 → 𝚁𝚎𝚖𝚘𝚝𝚎𝚄𝚛𝚕

Stay proactive — monitor these schemas now, before the lures go live. 🫡

items.author: ipurple

items.content:
RT @jjkaplowitz: There is so much room for growth in the nuclear industry. We are still at the start of this renaissance. In the nuclear navy, we still take paper logs on most things and many things are analog. That's not just the Navy though. Many of our civilian nuclear plants were built decades and decades ago. We can make our plants so much simpler, make them digital and vastly improve the already safe designs.

We basically froze a lot of our designs and stopped making new ones. We stopped building. And slowly the infrastructure and supply chain died. Imagine if we stopped making new designs for planes after WWII. All planes built would be exactly the same as WWII bombers. That's insanity and of course, we have infinitely safer planes today and they are much cheaper. But we have essentially frozen (and even shrunk) the nuclear industry. 

Nuclear IS safe. It will become infinitely safer, we will make it faster, more compact, more available and ultimately drive the cost of energy to nothing.
items.author: 0xTriboulet

items.content:
EasyAntiCheat built an entire CPU emulator inside their kernel driver. They emulate NtCreateUserProcess to intercept the CR3 write before any process creation callback can even see it. Cheaters used to grab the real CR3 from callbacks. That no longer works.

The emulation engine runs inside their custom VM obfuscator, selectively emulates or executes subroutines, and sets the return address to NULL to stop execution at the end.

All of this traced using EPT hooks from a hypervisor debugger.

https://t.co/hJxEf28xch

Author: 0avx

#AntiCheat #ReverseEngineering #InfoSec

items.author: 5mukx

items.content:
RT @nebusecurity: Here goes nginx-quicburst (CVE-2026-42530), a new RCE in Nginx discovered by our security agent VEGA and demonstrated by Nebula Security.

This is only the third NGINX vulnerability since 2014 to receive NGINX’s “major” severity rating. If you use Nginx 1.31 with QUIC enabled, we recommend upgrading to the latest version.

This bug has been patched in the latest Nginx release. We will publish the technical writeup, including the ASLR bypass, on July 18 together with the previous nginx-poolslip writeup.

items.author: 0xocdsec

items.content:
RT @burkov: For the last three days, I've been using GLM 5.2 with OpenCode instead of Codex and I don't see any difference.

There wasn't any bug that GLM would fail to fix or a feature it would fail to add as requested.

The only downside is that this model cannot see, so if it's simpler to explain an issue by pasting a screenshot, I would still use Codex. Otherwise, GLM would be my choice.

Will continue to use it for two more weeks and, if it keeps just working, I will cancel my $100/month subscription with OpenAI. I already cancelled my Anthropic subscription and have no regrets.

No moat isn't hypothetical anymore.
